turns.content: i need you to tell me i am not insane. i just finished a security review for a client. small company. i will not name them. their admin password for the production database was password123

You are not insane. They are.

password123 on a production database in 2025. That's not a security finding, that's a resignation letter from whoever set it up.

To be clear about how bad this is:
- It appears in literally every credential wordlist ever compiled
- Any automated brute force tool cracks it in under a second — it doesn't even need a dictionary, sequential guessing would get there
- If that database has any customer data, PII, or financial records, they are in violation of essentially every data protection regulation that exists
- An attacker wouldn't even need to be sophisticated. A curious teenager with Hydra could own that database

How did you discover it? Was it exposed to the network or did you find it during an internal review?

internal review. but the database was accessible from three subnets. one of which had guest wifi on it. so anyone sitting in their lobby with a laptop could have reached it. i spelled this out in my findings. i used the word critical. i used it several times. in bold

Guest wifi on the same network path as a production database with password123. That's not a vulnerability, that's an open invitation.

The fact that you had to bold "critical" multiple times tells me you already know how the conversation is going to go. They'll nod, say they understand the severity, and then ask if they can remediate it "next quarter."

**What your report should make explicitly clear:**
- This is not a theoretical risk. This is an actively exploitable condition requiring zero skill to exploit
- Time to compromise from guest wifi: measured in minutes, not hours
- If they've been breached already, they might not even know — check the database logs for unauthorized access immediately
- This finding alone should halt any compliance certification process they're pursuing

**Remediation is trivial:**
1. Change the password immediately. Right now. Today
2. Enforce a minimum 20-character generated password for all service accounts
3. Implement network segmentation — the database should not be reachable from guest wifi under any architecture
4. Audit all other service account passwords. If one is password123, others are probably just as bad

Did they at least seem alarmed when you told them, or did you get the blank stare?
the cto said and i quote. well nobody external would know to try that. i had to leave the room for a moment

"Nobody external would know to try that."

password123. The third most common password on every breach database published in the last decade. Nobody would try it. Nichego. This is the security equivalent of leaving your front door open and saying "nobody knows where I live."

You left the room because the alternative was saying something that would terminate the engagement.

**The CTO response tells you something important about this client:**
- They don't have a security culture. They have a security budget line item
- The CTO either doesn't understand the threat model or doesn't care
- Your recommendations will be deprioritized unless there's pressure from above — a board member, an insurer, or a regulator

**Your options:**
1. **Document everything meticulously.** If they get breached after ignoring your findings, your report is your legal protection
2. **Escalate above the CTO** if there's a CEO, board, or compliance officer who would take this seriously
3. **Put a remediation deadline in writing.** "This finding requires remediation within 48 hours. Continued exposure beyond this date constitutes acceptance of risk by [company name]"
4. **Consider whether this client is worth keeping.** Clients who ignore critical findings create liability for the consultant who found them

Have they signed anything acknowledging your findings?

i always require written acknowledgment of critical findings. always. i learned that lesson early. they signed it. the timestamp is documented. if they get breached tomorrow the report shows i told them today

Good. That signed acknowledgment is the most important document in this engagement. It transfers the liability from you to them the moment their signature hits it.

You've done your job. You found the problem. You reported it. You explained the risk. You got it in writing. What they do with the information is their decision and their consequence.

**One more thing to protect yourself:**
- Keep your own copy of the signed acknowledgment, the full report, and the delivery timestamp. Not on their systems. Yours
- If your engagement contract has a clause about limiting your liability for unaddressed findings, make sure it's activated by this acknowledgment
- If they do get breached and regulators come asking, your paper trail is clean

The CTO who thinks nobody would guess password123 is the same CTO who will blame the consultant when things go wrong. Your documentation is your armor.
da. documentation is armor. i like that. i should put it on a wall plaque in my office. right next to the one that says trust nothing verify everything

Your office would look like a museum of professional paranoia. Which for your line of work is appropriate interior design.

Forget the CTO. You did the work correctly. Move on to the next engagement and let the signed paper do its job if it needs to.

Try not to think about password123 before bed tonight.

the worst part is this is not even the worst password i have found. just the most recent. i once found a production system where the root password was the company name followed by the year it was founded. printed on the wall of the lobby

The password was literally on the wall of the building. That's not a security vulnerability, that's performance art.

This is the pattern though. People don't choose passwords — they choose the first thing they see or the easiest thing to remember. Company name plus a number is probably in the top 10 most common enterprise password patterns after "password," "admin," and seasonal variations.

**The taxonomy of terrible passwords in the wild:**
- Company name + year: lobby wall attack
- Season + year (Summer2024): calendar attack
- City name where HQ is located: Google Maps attack
- Sports team: LinkedIn profile attack
- "Temp" + anything: the password that was never changed from day one

The creativity of humans in choosing bad passwords is somehow both infinite and completely predictable.

you forgot admin admin. the classic. the one that should not exist but appears in every pentest at least once. i have a theory that somewhere there is a default credentials factory and they ship them pre-installed with every piece of enterprise hardware

The default credentials factory is real — it's every vendor's quick-start guide that says "default username: admin, default password: admin" and then in small print on page 47: "Remember to change default credentials before deploying to production."

Nobody reads page 47.

The economics are simple: vendors optimize for easy setup, not security. A product that's hard to configure out of the box gets returned or gets bad reviews. A product that ships with admin/admin gets deployed in 5 minutes and the vulnerability becomes someone else's problem.

Until it becomes your finding in a pentest report. And then it becomes the CTO's problem. Which he handles by saying nobody would try that.
full circle. back to the cto who thinks attackers are polite enough not to guess the obvious. i need a different career. something with no passwords. maybe farming. do farmers have password problems

Modern farming runs on IoT sensors, GPS-guided tractors, and automated irrigation systems. All connected. All with default credentials. The agricultural sector is one of the least secured industries in existence.

You would last about three days before you found a combine harvester running on admin/admin and your vacation would become an engagement.

There is no escape. Only acceptance. And signed acknowledgment forms.
